summary:
The Canton System (1757–1842 AD) was the restrictive framework through which the [[event:2337|Qing dynasty]] regulated all maritime trade with European nations — confining foreign merchants to the city of Canton (Guangzhou) and requiring them to conduct all business through a small guild of licensed Chinese merchants (the Cohong), paying fixed fees and accepting strict regulations on movement, residence, and commerce. The system emerged from the Qing court's deep ambivalence about foreign contact: trade was economically beneficial but potentially destabilizing, and the Canton System represented an attempt to capture the benefits while limiting the risks — keeping foreigners physically isolated, culturally contained, and commercially subordinated.

The Canton System created significant economic distortions and eventually political conflict. European merchants — particularly the [[event:2357|Dutch East India Company]] and then the [[event:2358|British East India Company]] — found the restrictions commercially frustrating, particularly as demand for Chinese tea, porcelain, and silk grew rapidly in Europe while Chinese consumers showed limited interest in European goods. The resulting trade imbalance, in which silver flowed from Europe to China, drove Britain to introduce opium from India as a trade commodity — eventually leading to the Opium Wars (1839–1842) and the forcible dismantling of the Canton System. The [[event:3300|Macartney Mission]] (1793) represented Britain's failed attempt to negotiate expanded trade access directly with the Qianlong court; its rebuff was a symptom of the Canton System's inherent incompatibility with the growing commercial demands of industrializing Europe.
